Interesting! Several observations for u8_gemv (the "fast" 1.6 TB/s one): - 108 PRMT (byte permutes - from u8 element extraction), 77 IMAD, 68 IADD3, 50 LOP3, 44 SHFL (!!), 20 LDS, 16 I2FP, 9 LDG, 5 BAR. SHFL + LDS + BAR indicate shared-memory round trips (layout conversions / the tl.sum reduction). Even the "fast" one has overhead. But the key difference: total SASS per loop iteration matters. Wait, these counts are totals for the whole kernel (loop bodies + prologue). Let me estimate per-iteration by looking at loop structure. Actually the important observation: the slow kernels aren't dominated by cvt at all — they have LOTS of PRMT, SHFL, LDS, BAR, FADD... **SHFL/LDS/BAR suggest Triton is moving tiles through shared memory / cross-lane shuffles in the loop.** For gemv_v8 (64,64) fp16 acc: per iteration: the acc += lo*xe[:,None] + hi*xo[:,None] shapes are (64,64) fp16... The xe[:, None] broadcast along axis1; if layout of acc has axis1 across threads that's fine... FADD 81 + HADD2 66 + HFMA2 25 + FFMA 32: the fp16 FMA acc should be HFMA2 only... lots of FADD (fp32 adds) from the `.to(tl.float32)` sum + sx sums. Honestly, this per-kernel aggregated view isn't conclusive about the loop body. Better: extract the loop body (between backward branches) and count instructions in the steady-state block. Let me disassemble with branch targets and find the hot block between BRA instructions. Alternative approach — much more direct: given the per-element instruction bloat in Triton on both paths, and the fundamental issue that Triton keeps inserting layout conversions for (KU, NB) fp16 acc with per-column broadcasts... maybe I should just write the inner loop differently: **Idea: keep everything as u32-packed and unroll fewer, wider loads with pure INT math on 32-bit lanes + fp32 FMA only at nibble level using PRMT-free extraction**: Per 4-bit weight the minimum work is: extract value (1-2 ops), fp multiply and add (2 ops). GPU-wide at 1.8TB/s → 18.6 weights/cyc/SM → ~75 ops/cyc/SM — well under 128. It's ACHIEVABLE if Triton emits the tight loop. The problem is purely Triton's codegen adding PRMT/SHFL/LDS garbage per tile element. Let me look at the actual loop body of gemv_v8 to understand the garbage. Actually, let me instead reconsider tile shapes: maybe the problem is that (64, NB) u8 tile with num_warps=4: 4096 elements / 128 threads = 32 elements/thread. Vectorized loads give 16B/thread × 2 loads. But then those 32 u8 per thread need extraction one-by-one — in SASS, extracting u8 #i from 4×b32 regs: PRMT per element pair... The u8 tensor ops in Triton on sm90: u8 & 0xF on u8 tensor → works on BYTE lanes?? SASS doesn't have byte ALU! Every u8 element lives in its own 32-bit reg after PRMT extraction from the loaded b32 → 32 PRMTs/thread just to unpack bytes, then per-nibble LOP3s, etc. **The fix: force the compiler to work on u32 elements**: load the weight tile as **int32** (KU, NB/4 with reinterpreted view? no wait — packed format: byte (k, n) holds k=2k,2k+1 of SAME column n. u32 at (k, n4) holds bytes for (8k..8k+7, n4)). If I load as u32 tile (KU, NBU) where NBU = NB/4... then w32 element = 4 consecutive n-bytes for 8 consecutive k. Extraction: lo4 = v & 0x0F0F0F0F (nibbles at byte positions), hi4 = (v>>4)&0x0F0F0F0F. Now STILL need fp16 conversion per weight... but here's the thing: the fp16-magic construction: value_for_nibble_j... Let me think again about constructing fp16x2 pairs from a u32: v = w32 (8 nibbles: weights w0..w7) lo = v & 0x0F0F0F0F → bytes = [w0, w2, w4, w6] hi = (v>>4) & 0x0F0F0F0F → bytes = [w1, w3, w5, w7] For fp16-magic on lo: want fp32 reg containing half2(w0, w2)... lo has byte0 = w0, byte1 = w2: `(lo & 0xFFFF) | 0x64006400` = half2(1024+w0, 1024+w2)!! LO=..bytes 0-1. And `((lo>>16) & 0xFFFF)|0x64006400` = half2(w4, w6)... so: - A = (lo & 0xFFFF) | 0x64006400 → LOP3 (lo, 0xFFFF, 0x6400...) → 1 instruction if LOP3 supports 3-input: LOP3.LUT(lo, 0xFFFF, 0x64006400): computes (a & b) | c → YES 1 instr - B = ((lo >> 16) | 0x64006400): SHF + LOP = 2 instr - C, D same from hi: 3 instr Total per u32 (8 weights): AND(lo) 1, SHF+AND(hi) 2, 4×LOP3+SHF = 6 → ~9-10 instr producing 4 half2 = 8 fp16 values. Then HFMA2: 8 weights × 1 FMA each = 4 HFMA2. Plus x broadcast... acc fp16x2 u32-form... But TRITON can't represent "u32-as-two-fp16" tensors. HOWEVER — WAIT. What if I just build the solution **in CUDA with load_inline** instead of Triton?? The rules explicitly allow: "one @triton.jit grid launch, or one CUDA __global__ built with load_inline". CUDA gives me FULL control: half2 intrinsics, __byte_perm, lop3, warp shuffles, cooperative grid sync (cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel + grid.sync()!). Grid sync is BUILT-IN and ROBUST (no spin-barrier hacks — actual cooperative launch ensures co-residency... wait my spin barrier works too; cooperative launch is cleaner and I can query max co-resident blocks with cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor properly). Writing the whole megakernel in CUDA is significantly more work but gives full control over the instruction stream, and honestly for int4 dequant GEMV, CUDA C++ is FAR easier to get right than coaxing Triton. The megakernel also has many phases → in CUDA that's just device functions + branch on blockIdx within one __global__. Plan: CUDA extension via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline at import/init time (compile ~30-60s once; cache dir under problem workspace? load_inline caches to TORCH_EXTENSIONS_DIR; the run env has cache/torch_extensions). - grid: cooperative launch, NCTA = SMs × blocksPerSM (query occupancy = 1 or 2). - Each phase = a __device__ function; phases separated by grid.sync() (cooperative groups). ~13 grid syncs per launch. - All weight pointers passed via __constant__ or a param struct (struct with ~150 pointers = 1.2KB — kernel param limit 4KB (32KB on newer?) — fine. Param space loads fast. - Per-step dynamic args: separate small struct (states + scratch + pos etc) — build per step on CPU, passed by value (params). We get compiled .so and call our own launcher that uses cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel — yes, we write the launcher C++ ourselves, using at::cuda streams. cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel is just CUDA runtime. This needs acc_j reset per group → the HSUB2 approach: acc contains Σ qx only (1024 already removedundefined ✓). Alternative: skip HSUB2, keep acc += (1024+q)*x, fold subtract (1024+z)*sx — BUT acc fp16 with offsets overflows precision as computed. UNLESS acc in FP32: HFMA2 cannot acc fp32... SO HSUB2 stays. OK 3.5 instr/weight → 65 ops/cyc/SMundefined ✓ under 128. OK here's my decision: **switch the whole megakernel to CUDA via load_inline.** This gives: 1. Full control of dequant inner loops (half2 magic, HFMA2, uint4 loads). 2. Real cooperative groups grid.sync() — robust barriers. 3. Explicit SMEM staging for x, scales, partials. 4. No Triton layout surprises. Effort is high but this is THE right call for a bandwidth-critical megakernel. Triton remains as fallback. Even better: tile cols 256 → row 256B = 32 lanes × 8Bundefined ✓ ONE row per warp-instruction (full warp coalesced 256Bundefined ✓undefined✓). item = 256 cols → N=16384/256 = 64 items over ~114-228 CTAs... eh: use 2 CTAs/SM = 228 CTAs, 64 items: imbalance (64/228 → most CTAs 1 item: 36% idle?? at least per-item streams 294KB — item-limited: phase time = 294KB/(SM with 2 CTAs... hmm items fewer than CTAs → 64 SMs busy → ~0.9 TB/s ceiling?? +"""CUDA microbench: fused int4 dequant GEMV with fp16 magic + uint2 loads."""
+import time
+import torch
+from torch.utils.cpp_extension import load_inline

+#include <torch/extension.h>
+#include <cuda.h>
+#include <cuda_fp16.h>
+#include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>
+
+// y[n] = sum_k x[k] * (q[k,n]-z[g,n])*s[g,n], x fp16, q int4 packed (K2,N) u8
+// item = 128 cols x k-half. 256 threads (8 warps). lane handles 8 cols x 2k per row.

Key design of the CUDA GEMV kernel: - 256 threads, item = 128 cols × k-split. - Shared memory holds x (fp16, K=2304) staged once per item?? better staged once per kernel for repeated items — but x staging per item costs K×2B per item... items per CTA few. Stage once at kernel start: 4.6KB. - Per lane: 4 cols (u32 per row per lane, 32 lanes × 4 cols = 128 cols). - Per row r (64 rows per group, 8 warps → r = r_base + warp + 8*i, 8 rows per warp per group). - Per u32: - lo = v & 0x0F0F0F0F; hi = (v>>4) & 0x0F0F0F0F (3 ops) - e01 = (lo | M) as half2, minus 1024 → __hsub2. Actually construction: lo has bytes [c0,c1,c2,c3]. lo | 0x64006400 → half2(1024+c0, 1024+c1) since low 16 bits = bytes c0 (bits 0-7), c1 (bits 8-15), each fitting in mantissa < 256undefinedundefined ✓. (lo>>8 | 0x64006400)?? NO — bytes c2,c3 are at bits 16-31: (lo>>16) | 0x64006400 gives half2(1024+c2, 1024+c3)undefinedundefined ✓. So: e01 = h2(lo|M) ; e23 = h2((lo>>16)|M); o01 = h2(hi|M); o23 = h2((hi>>16)|M). - I do need HSUB2 of 1024 per pair (4 HSUB2 per u32) OR keep offset & fold at group (fp16 overflow issue). Let me reconsider the fold with acc in FP32?? HFMA2 output is half2. t = e01*xe2 + o01*xo2 (fp16 mul + fma): values with 1024 offset: e01 ~ 1025..1039, xe ~ small: products ~ 10-3000 in fp16 — PRECISION loss as analyzed. SO DO HSUB2: 4 HSUB2. - after HSUB2: q values exactly: t01 = e01*xe2 + o01*xo2 (2 ops: 1 HMUL2 + 1 HFMA2), t23 same. - Now accumulate t01 into fp32 acc[0], acc[1] (lo/hi halves): extracting halves: __low2float/__high2float = 2 ops each?? That's cvt... hmm h2 → 2 floats: 2 F2F ops... per u32: t01,t23 → 4 float adds + 4 i2f-ish... adds ops back: per u32 (8 w): 3 (masks) + 4 (or-h2: LOP3|SHF... e01: LOP3(lo,M) = 1; e23: SHF+LOP3 = 2; o01/o23: hi already computed: 1+2 = 3 → 6) + 4 HSUB2 + 2 HMUL/HFMA... wait actually restructure to defer half extraction: BETTER: accumulate in half2 across the whole GROUP, extract to fp32 once per group: acc01_h2 (fp16x2 per colpair cols 0,1), acc23_h2 (cols 2,3): t01 = e01*xe2 + o01*xo2 → acc01 += t01 (HFMA2) — per u32: 1 HMUL2 + 2 HFMA2 (if reordered: acc01 = hfma2(e01, xe2, hfma2(o01, xo2, acc01)) = 2 HFMA2 required + t23 into acc23: 2 more. per u32 per-8-weights: masks 3 + or-construct 6 + hsub 4 + fma 4 = 17 instr → 2.1/weight. Group acc precision fp16: acc01 accumulates 64 rows... = Σ over 128 k?? no wait per acc-half: sums over 64 rows of same parity = 64 products (each ~ |q*x| ≤ 15·|x|): |acc| ~ √64·5 ~ 40: fp16 ulp 0.03 — good. Per group end: extract 4 cols: accf[j] = lo/hi float of acc01/acc23 (4 cvt), fold: y_part[j] = (accf[j] - zc_g * sx) * sc_g — wait acc01 holds Σ q*x over the group but across rows of BOTH parities?? e01*xe2 = q_even*xe, o01*xo2 = q_odd*xo — both parity contributions summed in same acc01 → acc01[j] = Σ_{g} q[k, c_j]*x[k] for both k paritiesundefinedundefined ✓ EXACTLY the group int-dot we wantundefinedundefined ✓. Then per group: y[n_j] partial accum in fp32 regs (no atomics until item done): gy[j] += (accf[j] - zc·sx)·sc; reset acc01/23 = 0 per group. Per-group overhead per lane: 4 cvt + ~10 fp ops + sc/zc loads (2×8B... sc row read: 4 bf16 per lane = 8B — hmm warp reads sc[grp, n0+4lane..]: lanes cover 128 cols × 2B = 256B per param per group: coalescedundefinedundefined ✓ but that's 2 extra loads/group/lane of 8B... fine, could vectorize but keep simple). Per group per lane instruction count: rows: 8 u32 loads (64 rows / 8 warps), each ~17 instr → 136 + fold ~20 = 156 instr per 8×8=64 weights?? per u32 = 8 weights → 64 weights/lane/group... wait: 8 rows × 8 weights = 64 weights per lane per group → 156/64 = 2.4 instr/weight — ok. At 1.8 TB/s: per SM 18.6 weights/cyc... per warp-instr covers 32 lanes: lane-ops needed = 18.6 × 2.4 = 45 lane-ops/cyc/SM = 1.4 warp-instr/cyc — trivial vs 4/cyc issueundefinedundefined ✓. Memory: to saturate, need in-flight ~11KB/SM: per lane 8B... hmm u32 loads: 8 rows × 4B apart... loads per group-window: issue 8 LDG.32 back-to-back (unrolled) = 8×128B/warp in flight = 1KB/warp × 8 warps = 8KB/SM (1 CTA) — close to 11KB; deeper unroll (2 groups interleaved) → 16KBundefinedundefined ✓. Compiler will pipeline the double-nested loop? Help with #pragma unroll on inner 8 + maybe prefetch. Measure first. sx per group: reading x from smem fp16 staged; compute per-warp redundantly: 128 H2F + adds = 130 instr per group per warp?? that's 130/64 = 2 instr/weight — DOUBLES work. Better: precompute sx ONCE per kernel start: 18 floats in smem (gsx[18]) as in my draft blockstart — yes: kernel-start staging computes gsx[g] once cooperativelyundefinedundefined ✓. But wait — with ksplit, item covers only HALF the rows: sx must be per group anyway (18 groups; item's k-half = groups 0-8 or 9-17)undefinedundefined ✓ same gsx table. Let me now write clean CUDA microbench. y accumulation via atomicAdd per item end (4 cols/lane × 32 lanes = 128 colsundefinedundefined ✓). x staged into smem fp16 (x prepared fp16 on host for microbench; real kernel stages bf16→fp16 in smem). Also alternative in the same file: int32-accumulator-free version... just measure this one first, plus a variant with KU unroll 2 groups. And measure across ksplit ∈ {1,2}, ncta ∈ {114, 228, 456}. Byte1 occupies bits [15:8] — its LOW 2 bits go into mantissa[9:8], and its HIGH bits CLOBBER THE EXPONENT. I made a huge blunder: OR-ing 0x64006400 works only when bytes are placed at fp16-mantissa positions that don't exceed 10 bits. For the LOW half: byte0 at bits[7:0]undefined ✓ fits mantissa[7:0], byte1 at bits [15:8] → spills into exponent!!! The REAL marlin trick uses PRMT to place bytes: extract c0 into a u16 (bits 0-7) and c1 into another u16, then concat: h2 = c0 | (c1 << 16): requires byte extraction: e01_u32 = (lo & 0xFF) ; e23_u32... i.e., one fp16 PER byte, not per 2 bytes unless we PRMT. For half2 packing [c0, c1]: need u32 = c0 | (c1<<16): from lo = [c0 c1 c2 c3] bytes: PRMT(lo, 0, sel) can place: byte c0 → position 0-1 (as u16 low), c1 → position 2-3: PRMT selector for [b0 at out-byte0, 0x64 at out-byte1?? PRMT sources bytes from two u32: prmt.b32 d, a=lo, b=0x6400?? PRMT result byte i = selected from {a0..a3, b0..b3} by nibble i of selector. Want out bytes = [lo.b0, 0x00?? wait fp16 (1024+c0) u16 = 0x6400 + c0: byte0 = c0 (mantissa bits 0-7... mantissa = c0 < 256 = 8 bits: 0x6400 | c0: byte0 = c0, byte1 = 0x64undefinedundefinedundefined ✓!! u16 = [c0, 0x64]. For half2: u32 = [c0, 0x64, c1, 0x64]: PRMT(a=lo, b=0x64646464, sel): out byte0 = a.byte0 (sel 0), out byte1 = b.byte? (sel 4+?...): b = 0x64 at all bytes: selector: out = [0, 4, 1, 4]?? = 0x4140 (hex nibbles read from out byte0): sel nibbles: [b0→out0]=0, [b.any64→out1]=4, [a.b1→out2]=1, [b→out3]=4 → sel = 0x4140. YES: **e01 = __byte_perm(lo, 0x64646464, 0x4140)** = half2(1024+c0, 1024+c1)exact!! Similarly e23 = __byte_perm(lo, 0x64646464, 0x4342), o01/o23 from hi. That's 1 PRMT per half2 (4 PRMT per u32 for 8 values... plus lo/hi computation 3 ops) — as cheap as intended. Then hsub2 = 4 more, fma 4 more, masked lo/hi 3 → per u32 (8 w): 3 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 15 instr. And no exponent clobberundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. My printf confirms: e01 low = 5136, not 4. The t0 bits: even the LOW half was wrong?? e01 low should have been... low half u16 = 0x6e04: byte1=0x0a clobbered → as shown. With PRMT both halves exact. Let me fix mb_cuda.py with __byte_perm and re-run.undefined

BUG!!! In gemv4: threads 0..17 read xs[] written by threads 0..255 concurrently — RACEundefinedundefinedundefinedundefinedundefined 🔴. gsx could read stale/unsynced values. In dbg5, gsx... dbg5 computes sx per group inside per-thread loop (each thread reads what others wrote... dbg5 stages xs with __syncthreads() after — yes has syncthreads before useundefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓). +z = (torch.rand(K // 128, N, device="cuda", dtype=torch.float32) * 8).to(torch.bfloat16)
+y = torch.zeros(N, device="cuda", dtype=torch.float32)
+dbg = torch.zeros(18, device="cuda", dtype=torch.float32)

Analyzed ideal: GEMV has to also read scales/zeros (6%) + write y via ATOMICS + read x. The measured ~1.0 TB/s is barely half of copy. The compute per weight is ~2.5 instr/weight → at 18.6 weights/cyc/SM that's ~46 lane-ops/cyc — should NOT cap at 1.0 TB/s... unless LDS/issue slots or the memory access pattern hurts: each warp reads 2 rows of 128B per instruction (2×128B segments, 4 sectors) vs copy reading full 128B per warp fully-coalesced-4×32B. In-flight: per warp 4 uint2 loads per group-pair... in-flight bytes: per group: lanes' loads = 8 uint2 (per lane 4) → per warp 8×32... per lane 4 loads×8B = 32B × 32 lanes... per warp per group: 4 loads/lane * 256B = 1KB in flight... 16 warps → 16KB/SM. At 700ns latency and 15.8 GB/s/SM needed: 11KB — roughly there. Occupancy 25% marginal. Increase: 1. **More CTAs/SM**: 3-4 CTAs (24-32 warps = 37-50% occ). Items: (N/128)*ksplit... with ksplit=2: 256 items for 114 CTAs → can't fill 3-4 CTAs/SM (would need 342-456 CTAs = items 256 → CTAs get <1 item... imbalanced). Split N more finely? item 64 cols × ksplit...: 64 cols → lane covers 4 cols?? load uint2 8B = 8 cols... 64-col items: 16 lanes cover... whatever — more splits = more flexibility. Easier: ksplit up with smaller cols... but ksplit hurts (saw ks3 slower — why? per-item work shrinks → startup w/ gsx staging amortized worse!! gsx/x staging is per-CTA PER KERNEL (not per item — good) but the SCALES bring extra traffic per item (128 cols × 18 groups × 2 params × 2B = 9.2KB per item vs weights 73.7KB per k-half item → 12% with ks2, 18% with ks3...). Also more atomic traffic for y. 2. **Reduce staging cost**: it reads full x (2304×2B per CTA — 4.6KB) + computes gsx — only once per CTA per launch. Fine. 3. **Bigger tiles per CTA to push efficiency**: n-items 128-col → the K-loop is the stream; processes 2 rows/instr... could do 4 rows × 16 lanes... deeper unroll (process 2 groups per outer iter: 8 loads in flight/lane). 4. **The sc/zc loads**: per group per lane 2 loads LDG... they're L1-resident (each (grp, cbyte..+8): 16B per param per lane per group — L1 hit after first group? sv0/zv0 are per grp → new each group. 9.2KB per item from L2. Extra ~12% traffic explains part of gap vs copy. 5. **Atomics for y**: 128 floats per item per... with ks2: 512 items... wait nitems = 256 for ks2: each does 8 atomicAdd/lane × 32 lanes... total atomic fp32 = 256 items×256 thr×8/... eh = 256×2048 = 524K atomics → hmm that's significant: 524288 × 4B = 2MB red traffic. RED f32 ~ maybe 1-2 µs. Could reduce by ksplit design. Actually — reduce by having ksplit=1 (no atomics duplicate per column... ks1: each col written by 2 lanes... wait in m7 each col gets written by lanes sharing it: 2 per warp × 8 warps = 16 lanes → 16 atomics per col per item!! With k-split ks: ×ks. y atomics = N × 16 × ks... ks1: 16×16384 = 262K. Meh. Reduce: write PARTIAL sums to distinct y buffers?? no — atomics are into L2, cheap-ish. Honestly, ~1.0 TB/s might be close to what this will do. But theoretical says compute should fit. (b) memory pipeline: uint2 loads of 8B per lane × 16 lanes contiguous → 128B segments; each warp-instr covers 2×128B (rows r, r+1 — N=16KB apart!!) → 2 separate 128B segments per instruction — fine, both full sectors. Per SM per cycle sectors: 8 sectors/warp-instr... need LDG rate: bytes/SM/cyc at 1.8TB: 9.3B → sectors 0.29/cyc → LDG-warp-instr rate 0.036/cyc — tiny. So memory-instr rate trivial; it's LATENCY-BOUND: in-flight bytes per SM insufficient! In-flight estimate for m7-ks1-ncta228 (2 CTAs/SM): per lane per group: 4 uint2 loads (interspread with dependent math): the 4 loads are in an unrolled pp-loop → issue together-ish (4 loads × 8B = 32B/lane) → per warp 1KB → 16 warps = 16KB/SM → covers 16KB/700ns = 23 GB/s/SMundefined ≈ 2.5 TB/s aggregate IF perfectly pipelined (loads always in flight). AGGREGATE = 16 warps×32B/700ns = 11.7 GB/s... × wait per SM: 512B in flight?? I need to be careful: per warp: 4 loads of 8B = 32B... the lane executes: 4 loads, then DEPENDENT math (needs load 1 data) → stall ~700ns → math 240 cyc → next group. Per warp: 32B per ~ (700ns+240cyc=840ns@1.7GHz) → 38 MB/s?? no wait that can't be right — 8B per lane per load × 32 lanes = 256B per load-instr — 4 loads = 1KB per warp in flight per group iteration: 1KB/0.84µs = 1.2 GB/s per warp × 16 warps = 19 GB/s per SM × 114 = 2.2 TB/s ceiling. So latency analysis says ~ 2.2 max if math fully overlaps... Math DOESN't overlap: the dependent chain means math time adds. To break the serialization: issue NEXT group's loads BEFORE current math → explicit prefetch of the next uint2s. STRUCTURE with explicit prefetch: ``` uint2 cur[4]; sv,zv cur... load cur for g=0 for g: load next (g+1) weights + sz (indep) math on cur cur = next ``` The loads issue BEFORE the math of the same iteration → memory latency overlaps math of PREVIOUS iteration... in-order issue: iteration g: LDG(g+1) issues before math(g) which stalls on LDG(g)... LDG(g+1) already committed to pipelineundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ dependencies: math(g) needs LDG(g) (in flight long). With prefetch depth 1, in-flightundefined ≈ 2× (current computing + next loading) → ~2x improvement. Deeper: prefetch 2 groups. Actually, better idea to consider — **use async copy (cp.async) / __pipeline primitives to move weights into SMEM**: cp.async bypasses registers, 16B per thread per op, deep pipelines without register pressure — THE standard for this on Ampere+. With weights in SMEM, the unpack math reads from SMEM (cheap). Pipeline: stages of whole groups: per group per CTA: 128B/row × 64 rows = 8KB. Double-buffer: cp.async 8KB → wait → math. Splitting equally 8-ways across warps is FINE (any partition summing to 1× works — the subtraction is linear), BUT there's a subtlety with mb4 layout: each column appears in EXACTLY ONE lane per warp and each warp has lanes covering DIFFERENT rows — but zacc per lane accumulates zc*sx*sc over ALL groups. reasoning
relerr=1e-3 correct. 1.08-1.15 TB/s. The prefetch pipeline isn't helping latency much. Frustrating plateau. Let me try the **cp.async to shared memory approach** — that's the heavyweight tool: 16B per lane async copies without register round-trip, deep pipeline via commit groups. Structure: per item, stream into SMEM ring buffer of G groups (each group = 8KB for 128-col tile); consumer processes groups out of SMEM. Compute reads u32 from SMEM (LDS). Actually — before cp.async, quick assessment of the fundamental per-SM throughput issue. Let me try increasing CTAs to use more warps: ncta up to 912 (8/SM)... mb4 layout with ncta 912 needs items: ks=... (N/128)=128 × ks=... ks=9 → 1152 items / 912 — fine. Tried ks=9/456 = slow (per-item overheads). Hmm ks=9: rows_per=128, ng=2/... the per-item overhead: zacc, gy init, atomics — amortized over fewer rows = wasteful + scale traffic share grows. Different idea: lane covers 8 cols (uint2 loads instead of u32) → same as m7. Or process TWO rows per lane per instruction group... Actually, let me try cp.async now — it's the canonical answer for these kernels and buys: (1) LDG replaced by cp.async.cg (bypasses L1/register), (2) deep pipelining trivial, (3) frees registers. Structure per CTA-item: - SMEM ring: STAGES × 8KB (one group = 64 rows × 128B) + wait... for 128 cols: 64 rows × 128B = 8KB per group. 4 stages = 32KB SMEM (fine at 228KB). cp.async: per group stage: 8KB / 256 threads = 32B/lane = 2 cp.async of 16B each. - Producer: for g in 0..ng-1: wait stage free (cp.async.wait_group), issue cp.async for group g+D-1. - Consumer: read u32 from SMEM: lane mapping (row r = warp + 8rr, byte 4*lane): address in smem = (stage_base + r*128 + 4*lane): LDS.32 — bank conflicts? threads in warp read consecutive u32 (lane*4 bytes) → 32×4B = 128B across banks... perfect stride-1: conflict-freeundefinedundefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. - x reads stay from SMEM xs too. cp.async requires 16B alignment: 8KB stage offsets alignedundefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ cp.async.cg 16B per op: lane covers 2 ops: bytes (lane*16, lane*16+16)?? 256 lanes... wait cp.async.ca/.cg per thread 16B: 256 threads × 16B = 4KB per issue round → 2 rounds per 8KB groupundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. Row mapping: the 8KB group slab = rows 64 × 128B contiguous — global addresses: row r at w+(rbase+r)*N + n0: stride N bytes!! NOT contiguous in gmem — 64 separate 128B segments — cp.async per segment of 128B = 8 threads × 16B: assign thread t → (row t/8 within group + ..., seg t%8): 64 rows × 8 segs = 512 ops over 256 threads = 2 eachundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. Let me write this (mb9). Also compare a SIMPLER alternative in the same file: double-buffer via SMEM written with NORMAL LDG+STS (no cp.async) — nah cp.async is the point. Also — think: is there an even better idea?? **Just read via L2 more cleverly**: the loads ARE coalesced; latency-bound because of dependent math between loads. cp.async decouples by having MANY copies outstanding. Yes, do mb9. Detail: __pipeline primitives: use intrinsics from <cuda_pipeline.h>: __pipeline_memcpy_async(dst_smem, src_gmem, 16); __pipeline_commit(); __pipeline_wait_prior(n);. Available sm80+. Wait careful about which operand: qlat[h, c] = Σ_dn q_nope[h,dn] * W[c, h*256+dn] where W = kv_b (K=512 the c-dim, N=8192). GEMV: "x" = q_nope[h] (128 values, fp32 in scratch → fp16), weight kv_b packed (256, 8192), output cols h*256..h*256+128. BUT — the y output of this GEMV is INDEXED BY c (the 512 K-dim of kv_b!!) NO WAIT — REREAD: outputs are over dn (128 columns of kv_b for head h's nope part)?? qlat[h,c]: c = 512 latent index: c indexes ROWS of kv_b!!! NOT columns! qlat[h,c] = Σ_dn q_nope[h,dn] * kv_b[c, h*256+dn] — output per ROW c of kv_b — that's a GEMV along the WRONG AXIS!! The weight layout (K=512, N=8192) row-major: y2[dn] = Σ_c x2[c] * kv_b[c, dn] is the natural GEMV (x2 over rows, output over columns). But qlat wants outputs over ROWS with input over COLUMNS: qlat = q_nope @ kv_b^T restricted to 128 columns... = row-vector @ (128-col slice) transposed: qlat[c] = dot(kv_b[c, n0:n0+128], q_nope[h]) → per-row dot over 128?? NO WAIT: kv_b[c, :] indexing: first dim c is K (in=512), second is N=8192 (out). kv_b[c, col]: row c, column col. qlat[h,c] = Σ_dn q_nope[h,dn]·kv_b[c, h·256+dn]: summing over COLUMNS (dn direction) for fixed row c!! That is y[c] = Σ_d kv_bT?? this is a GEMV over the column direction: treating kv_b TRANSPOSED: (q_nope[h] as x over columns-dn of the slice, outputs = rows c). Transposed GEMV: outputs over the K-dim (rows) — each output row c = dot(row c's 128 cols, q_nope). Row-major streaming: row c = 128 contiguous bytes... wait kv_b row = 8192 bytes; the slice = 128 bytes contiguous. Each output needs 128B sequential → PERFECT for the "lane splits columns" pattern but with roles flipped: output per ROW. Transposed GEMV with int4: pack format packs along K (rows→c)?? packed along `in` = K = c-dim: byte (c/2, dn) holds (c=2r, 2r+1) for column dn — i.e. nibbles are along c — SAME column! So a packed byte (r, n) = weights for (c=2r, n) and (c=2r+1, n) — for the transposed GEMV output[c] over dn-sums: output[2r] = Σ_dn q[dn] * lo(r, n); output[2r+1] = Σ q[dn]*hi(r,n). Standard GEMV form again!! with roles: "outputs" = rows (c, the pack dim!), "inputs" = columns n (dn slice). Dequant params: scales indexed by (c//128, n): group g = 64 packed rows... per output row c: scale[c//128, n] — varies with BOTH c and n. Task: output[2r] = Σ_n lo(r,n)*sc[r/64, n]*q[n] - ... per packed row pair. Let item = 64 rows (c) × full 128-col slice?? Stream per row: 128B; per output c: dot over 128 cols (128B = 64 u32: lanes cover 2 cols each?? hmm. SIMPLEST mapping: warp per row c: lane covers 4 cols (u32): 32 lanes × 4 = 128undefinedundefinedundefined ✓ per lane: u32 (r, 4l) = weights (c=2r, cols 4l..4l+3... wait — hold on, the UNIT is (r, n): r = c/2: row byte at (r, n): nibble lo = (c=2r). lane u32 = (r, 4l..4l+3) → for output (2r): lo nibbles × q[4l+j]; hi → output 2r+1. acc per lane for out_even/out_odd: 2 values × ... lane reduces over its 4 cols: warp-reduce over 32 lanes → output[2r], output[2r+1] — warp reduction per row (shfl) = 5-step butterfly: per row ~ 5 shfl × 2. Per item (head h): 256 packed rows → 256 warp-rows / 8 warps = 32 rows each: stream per row 128B — full warp coalescedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ per row: lane u32 load + extract + 2 fma·... + butterfly reduce + sc/zc: sc[g, n]: g = r/64 (c//128): c = 2r → g = (2r)/128 = r/64undefinedundefined ✓ per row r: scale row g applies BOTH parities (c=2r, 2r+1 both in group g when... c=2r: group c//128 = 2r//128 = r//64undefinedundefined ✓undefinedundefined ✓): zsc fold: out_e = (Σ q lo - z? with sx: corrections per (g,n): sx_nope = Σ q[h, dn] — constant per item!! fold: out[2r] = qdot·... - z[g,n]*sxq*sc... wait careful: (Σ_n lo(n)*sc(n)*q(n)) - Σ_n z(n)sc(n)*q(n) = Σ (lo - z)sc q per element — CAN'T factor via sx unless sc constant per... the z-sc product: Σ_n z[g,n]*sc[g,n]*q[n] = another dot over the SAME terms! = dot(zsc_row, q) — NOT sx*q — different from stream GEMV case!! In stream GEMV (outputs over n): x is broadcast per-k, so Σ_k x q[k,n] with per-n zc: z_term = z[n]*sc[n]*Σ_k x[n] = zsc*sx — factored because z/sc const along k. HERE outputs over c (=k-ish direction for qlat): z/sc vary along the OUTPUT dim... per output c: z_term = Σ_n z[g,n]sc[g,n]q[n] — INDEPENDENT of c except through g!! → precompute per group: zsq[g] = Σ_n zsc[g,n]*q[n] ONCE per item (128 terms × ng=4 groups — cheap): out[c] = qdot_c*... no wait: out[c] = Σ_n (lo(r(c),n) - z[g,n])*sc[g,n]*q[n] = [Σ_n lo·sc·q] - [Σ_n z·sc·q:g] — build scq[n] = sc[g,n]*q[n] per group (fp16 vectors in smem), then out_e = Σ lo·scq - zsq[g]: the MAIN dot per output-row pair via lo/hi nibbles against scq (per group, loaded into smem per group: 128 fp16 × 4 groups = 1KB) — and the zsq correction precomputed. Per packed row r: lane: 4 cols: u32 → 8 nibbles → 2 dots vs scq[4l..+4]: acc_e += Σ_j lo_j*scq_j fp16 fma ×4 + acc_o similarly; g boundaries: scq for group r/64... per item 4 groups → scq reloading every 64 rows — fine. OK — manageable, BUT this "transposed" GEMV is structurally different from the stream GEMV. NOTE the same issue arises for **kv_b in absorbed form** and for **W_v** (o = o_lat·... wait: o_head[d] = Σ_c o_lat[h,c] * kv_b[c, h*256+128+d]: sum over ROWS (c), output over COLUMNS (d) — STANDARD stream GEMV direction (like o_proj)undefinedundefinedundefined ✓ outputs over N=d, inputs over K=cundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ GOOD — only qlat is transposed. So qlat[h] = kv_b[:, cols]^T... (128 cols) → qlat (512,). The GEMV: inputs = q_nope (128, along columns-of-slice), weight = kv_b transposed-slice (8192-col slice → 128 cols × 512 rows), outputs = 512 c-values. AS ANALYZED. transposed GEMV. Right. For (B), nibbles along OUTPUT dim — as analyzed with per-output-pair structure. - attention items (chunk, hg=2): per item: rows [c0, c1) of c_kv (+k_rope): per lane... Now — CRITICAL final structure decision: the phase layout per block: KDA block (P1 GEMV | P2 recurrence+o_proj | P3 MoE): 3 grid.syncs. MLA block (P1 GEMV | P2a qlat+attn | P2b combine+Wv+o_proj | P3 MoE): 4 syncs. Total: 3×3 + 4 = 13 grid.sync + end. Plus per-launch prologue. x flow: x_in (bf16 gmem) for block0; P3 writes out_moe → next block P1 reads as fp32; final P3 → x_out bf16. KDA P2 writes out_attn (+residual). Wait — h for the MoE norm: h = out_attn; moe input = rmsnorm(out_attn, moe_norm) — computed redundantly per CTA in P3 from out_attn (gmem fp32)undefinedundefined ✓. And out_moe = out_attn + moe_outresidual... wait no: h_new = h + moe_out where h = out_attn. out_moe accumulates expert outputs (zeroed in P2) + each CTA adds out_attn slice in P3 (the residual)undefinedundefined ✓ as designed before. Attention kv-cache registry & args: per step build the C++-side arg struct — actually pass everything in ONE param struct: static weight ptrs (~150) + per-step state ptrs + scratch + scalars. Kernel param limit: sm90: 32KB — struct ~1.5KB fine. Host builds the struct per step: static part PREBUILT once (memcpy a prebuilt blob), dynamic part patched per step → struct copy per step on CPU (~1 µs) → pass by value. Launch: cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel once per step. Verify cooperative launch support & co-residency: grid = 228 = 2/SM — check cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor ≥ 2 at runtime; fallback to 114 grid. Python overhead per step: dict lookups + building arg struct (python ~ 5-20 µs) + launch (~10 µs). GPU ~ 200µs → CPU stays aheadundefinedundefined ✓. fp16 activation staging everywhere (xs fp16): rmsnorm outputs fp16 — hmm PRECISION: x bf16 → rmsnorm fp32 → fp16: fp16 rounding 2^-11=5e-4 — fine. And reference rounds to bf16 (2^-8=4e-3) — mine is MORE preciseundefinedundefined ✓. But careful: fp16 RANGE: normed values ~O(1)undefinedundefined ✓ + conv-state values init ~ 0.1undefinedundefined ✓, S ~ 0.05undefinedundefined ✓, qlat — used in fp32-defined paths (attention via mma fp32 acc) — inputs bf16 c_kv, qlat fp32 → cast to bf16 for mma... qlat values ~ Σ 128 q·w ~ 128·0.02·1 ~ 2.5, fine as bf16 (0.4% err per value — error in scores ~ relative 0.4%... softmax sensitive to exp(s·Δ): score magnitudes ~ ±3-5·scale... exp amplification of 0.4% score error — score err abs ~ 0.02 → probability err ~ 2%?? hmm: s·err... exp(s_true+δ) = exp(s_true)(1+δ): δ=0.02 → 2% rel on p — then o = Σ p·v: errors partially cancel... cosine of out vs ref: well above 0.98? Probably yes but let me keep qlat→mma in fp16→bf16... to be safer: could keep scores in higher precision by doing c_kv @ qlat in fp32 for... no — that kills tensor cores. Just test: cosine gate 0.98 on FULL hidden output; one attention layer's 2%-probability error propagating to output: output is a sum of many terms; cosine impact small. PROCEED, measure, adjust if fails. fp16 for xs: another subtlety — o_proj input o: reference computes o fp32 → CAST TO BF16 → GEMV over bf16 o. Mine: o fp32 → cast fp16 (or bf16?) for GEMV. The trick WORKS IN FP16 ONLY (0x6400|q = 1024+q undefinedundefinedundefined ✓; bf16 cannot represent 1024+q exactly). For xs: x rmsnormed → fp16 (better than reference's bf16)undefinedundefined ✓ SAFE. For c_kv input to mma: it's bf16 ALREADY (cache)undefinedundefinedundefined ✓ mma.bf16undefinedundefined ✓ NICE. qlat: computed fp32 → cast to bf16 for the mma B-side: err ~ 0.4% relativeundefined fine. o_accumulation (o_lat) via mma with p as bf16 A-operand: pundefined∈[0,1] bf16: err 0.4% per element... sums fine. k_rope mma: q_rope bf16 (rope'd then stored to scratch as fp32→cast bf16)undefinedundefined ✓ c-side k_rope cache bf16undefinedundefined ✓. Per warp: for token block T (e.g., 16 tokens at a time): - load c_kv block (16 tokens × 512): A-operand for score dot... Warp w ∈ 0..7: assign (headgroup-8 hg8 = w/1??) 8 warps: 2 hg8-groups × 4 warps: warps 0-3 → heads hg*16..+8, warps 4-7 → +8..+16: each warp-group-of-... hmm but then 4 warps redundantly loading same A (c_kv tokens)?? Share via smem staging: stage T tokens × 512 c (fp16... bf16) into smem (T=16: 16KB), all warps read fragments via ldmatrix. Per token-block of 16: A (16×512) read from gmem → smem (vectorized uint4 ldg → sts), then per head-octet: mma (16, 512)@(512, 8): 32 mma.k16 steps → accumulate scores (16, 8) fp32 in regs (2 per lane since 16×8/32=4/lane... m16n8 fp32 frag = 4/lane). rope part: (16×64)@(64,8): 4 mma steps. softmax on (16,8) per column... updates o_acc (8 heads c-dim... o_acc per warp-octet? o_acc (8 heads, 512) fp32 — per CTA 16 heads → 2 octets: total o_acc regs per warp = assign each warp OWNERSHIP of (octet, c-slice) for the output accumulation instead: o_acc accumulate via ANOTHER mma: (8 heads... o_acc[h, c] += Σ_t p[t,h]·c_kv[t,c]: mma: A = p^T (8, 16) bf16... m16n8k16: M=8?? min M 16: use m16n8k16 with M=16 = 2 octets stacked?? A (16,16) = [p_oct0^T; p_oct1^T] (16 rows = 16 heads), B (16, 8) = c_kv block columns (t, c-slice 8): output (16 heads, 8 c) per k-step... iterate c in 8-steps: 64 mma steps per token-block. Each warp: c-range 512/8 = 64 values → 8 k-steps of (16,16)@(16,8): warp w owns c-slice [w*64, +64): BUT mma B operand = c_kv col values for its slice — same A (p) across warps (share via smem/regs broadcast—p^T fragments identical per warp: load from smem or shfl). o_acc fragments per warp: (16, 8)×8 steps = 16×64 fp32 = 1024 / 32 lanes = 32/laneundefinedundefined ✓ as planned. Rescale per token-block: o_acc *= f (per head): multiply the fragments by per-head factor: lane's fragment rows map to heads... m16n8 frag: lane holds (row groups): scale rows by f[row]: each lane's 4-element frag per (16,8): rows r0, r0+8: f lookups ×4 per step × 8 steps — ok-ish. Alternatively TWO-PASS softmax: scores → recompute after chunk?? no — online is fine with the rescale overhead (~1 rescale per block × rare max-updates... must apply EVERY block (f = exp(m_old - m_new)): yes every block, ~ (4 frags × 8 steps × 1 op) + exp computations... fine. Hmm, the rescale-mma cost per token block: rescale multiplies o_acc frags (32/lane... 32 ops) + mma 8 steps + scores mma (32 steps/octet... across 8 warps: scores split: 2 octets × 32 steps over... wait scores mma per octet over full 512: 32 k-steps: assign warps 0-3 → oct0 k-splits (0..7, 8..15, 16..23, 24..31)?? then scores parts must SUM across 4 warps — cross-warp reduce via smem/atomic!! UGH. Alternative simpler split: warps 0-3 for oct0 scores (covering k in quarters each, then reduce 4 partials by adding in smem)... RESTRUCTURE to make life simpler: scores mma: A = c_kv (16 tokens, 512) smem-staged; B = qlat (512, 16 heads) — B columns 16 heads: 2 mma-N-octets (n8): per token-block: 2 octets × 32 ksteps = 64 mma ops per CTA — assign by (octet, k-quarter): warp w: octet w%2... 8 warps: (oct, kq) pairs = 2×4: warp covers 8 k-steps: partial scores (16,8) per warp → 4 partials per octet to reduce. Reduce: after mma loop, warps add partials into smem scores (16,16) fp32 (atomic-free: staged per warp then summed, or atomicAdd to smem — smem atomics f32 exist (atomicAdd on shared: yes undefined). smem traffic small (16×16×4B×8 partials = 8KB). Then ONE warp-group computes softmax updates (m, z, p) for (16 tokens × 16 heads) and rescale factors → into smem; ALSO computing p bf16 for the o mma. Then o_acc mma as above with c-split across warps (no reduction needed — each warp owns its c-slice's o_acc: (16, 64slice) fragments: p^T fragments needed per warp: from smem bf16 p^T (16 heads × 16 tokens) — B-operand = c_kv (16, 8 slice) from smem (ldmatrix)undefinedundefined ✓ A = p^T (16h, 16t) : mma A row-major... m16n8k16: M=16 heads, K=16 tokens, N=8 c. A fragments row-major from smem. OK. Cross-check register: score frags (16,8)/32 = 4/lane; o_acc 32/lane. Token-block size 16 gives scores (16,16) mma — fine. This is a LOT of mma plumbing, but standard. Timeframe: attention phase maybe 8-15 hours of work total for me. Fine. Actually — WAIT. Let me simplify deeply: do I even need mma for the attention score dot? Reconsider CUDA-core fp16 path: 268 MFLOP at fp32-fma rate ~ (114 SM × 128 lanes × 1.7GHz) = 24.8 T-FMA/s: 268M×... per side includes o_acc (another L×512×32) → total ~ 537 MFLOP fp32 → 21.6 µs at 16k ctx. At 2k ctx: 2.7 µs. Geomean impact vs mma (0.7 µs): at 16k mma saves 20 µs on a ~200 µs stepundefined ≈ 10%; geomean over (2k saves 2µs, 8k saves 8, 16k 20) → ~ 3-4% geomean score. hfma2 halves the non-mma cost. MMA saves more but costs big complexity AND shared-memory bandwidth (staging c_kv through smem 16KB per 16 tokens: staged once per item: total L×1KB×2hg = 2× c_kv traffic through smem — writes+reads; smem BW is huge, fine). Alternative middle path: **fp32 dot but with the c-split accumulation across warps removed**: each warp computes FULL 16-head scores for its (token-block, c-slice) and keeps PARTIAL scores; after chunk completes... no wait softmax needs full-c scores per token. What about making score-compute cheaper: c_kv is bf16! fp16 HFMA2 with bf16→fp16 conversion of c_kv... bf16 (exp 8) → fp16 (exp 5): values ~0.1-3 fine range → convert c_kv chunk to fp16 in smem during staging (cvt cost once per element: L×512×1 cvt — cheap). Then scores via hfma2 fp16×fp16→fp32?? NO fp16 FMA → fp16 acc only (HFMA2). fp16 acc for 512-term dots: q·c sums ~ ±30, err ~ √512·0.05?? borderline. fp32 acc needed for precision. CUDA fp16→fp32 mixed-precision FMA: HFMA2 with f32 acc?? doesn't exist on SM90 outside mma. Decision: **mma for attention** (it's the right answer), with c_kv smem staging converting nothing (mma.bf16 reads bf16 directly). qlat → bf16, fine. OK – I've spent a lot of thinking; time to BUILD. Plan the actual files: solution.py structure: ```python import os, torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F from dataclasses import dataclass, field from torch.utils.cpp_extension import load_inline # constants GROUP_SIZE = 128, EPS... # CUDA source (megakernel) as string; load_inline at import: compile once. # QuantLinear, QuantExperts, KDA, MLA, MoE, Block, Model replicating reference structure # with identical buffer names. Model.step: lazy prep (build param struct blob), then # cache mgmt + single cooperative launch. ``` Model class must be an nn.Module with: - blocks: ModuleList(Block) each with attn_norm, moe_norm, attn (KDA/MLA with q_proj etc QuantLinear), moe (router, gate/up/down QuantExperts, s_*) - step(hidden, state): the megakernel path. For load_state_dict(strict=True), the parameter/buffer names must match reference exactly — copy reference's module tree verbatim (init methods), then Model.reset_parameters NOT needed (weights come from load_state_dict; but benchmark also calls solution.Model(cfg) then load_state_dict — need buffers registered with right shapes: fine). BUT WATCH OUT: reference Model.__init__ calls reset_parameters() which INITIALIZES ALL QUANT BUFFERS via reference quantize() — expensive (~seconds). My solution Model can register empty buffers WITHOUT init (load_state_dict overwrites). Quantize import not needed — just shapes. state_dict keys structure: `blocks.0.attn.q_proj.w_q` etc. KDA attrs: q_proj/k_proj/v_proj/g_proj (QuantLinear), beta_proj (nn.Linear weight), conv_w (Parameter), o_proj, attn_norm, moe_norm, moe.router.weight, moe.gate.w_q..., moe.s_gate... MLA: attn.q_proj, kv_a, kv_b, o_proj.undefined ✓ copy names exactly. Prep step: after load_state_dict, on FIRST step() call: build: - weight pointer table (CPU list of ints), embed in param struct. - scratch buffers (torch.empty on device): proj scratch fp32 (16416), partial scratch (4.2MB), out_attn/out_moe (2304×2), barrier isn't needed (cooperative grid.sync()!!), small fp32 areas (beta 32, qrope 2048+64+512, qlat 16384...). - cache registry dicts. - launch config: grid = ? (query occupancy via a probe or use 2×SMs and verify cooperative launch doesn't error; actually co-residency: use cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor on the compiled kernel → blocks_per_sm = value; grid = that × SMs clipped to ≥1). If occupancy returns 2: grid 228. if 1: 114. The kernel arg struct: pass ~150 static pointers. I'll pass ONE struct by value: struct MegaParams { const uint8_t* w[80]; const bf16* wf[40]; float* scratch[8]; uint8_t* state[24]; ... int pos; int copy_rows; int nchunk; ... } — design fields per use. +Stage A skeleton: reference-identical module tree (buffer/param names match
+reference.py exactly so load_state_dict works), an eager oracle step for
+validation, and the megakernel path wired in progressively.

+from __future__ import annotations

+import os
+from dataclasses import dataclass, field
+
+import torch
+import torch.nn as nn

 """Solution: Kimi-Linear W4A16 hybrid decode, single-launch CUDA megakernel.
 
-Stage A skeleton: reference-identical module tree (buffer/param names match
-reference.py exactly so load_state_dict works), an eager oracle step for
-validation, and the megakernel path wired in progressively.
+The decode step runs entirely as ONE cooperative CUDA kernel launch:
+  - all int4 dequant-GEMVs fused (unpack -> fp16 magic -> HFMA2, streamed once)
+  - KDA recurrence + conv + o_proj, MLA absorbed attention + o_proj, MoE chain
+  - RMSNorms and residual adds, driven by grid.sync() phase barriers.
+
+This file also keeps an exact eager oracle (`_step_eager`) used during bring-up.
+Module tree mirrors reference.py so reference state_dict loads unchanged.
 """

No — params by value is fine. Let me organize. // KDA layers x3, each: // wq,wk,wv,wg,wo (uint8*), sq,sk,sv,sg,so (bf16*), zq..zo, beta (bf16*), conv (bf16*), attn_norm, moe_norm, router, // moe: gate/up/down w/s/z (3x3), shared g/u/d w/s/z (3x3) -> 15 quant + 1 beta + 1 conv + 2 norms + 1 router = 20 ptrs // MLA: q,kva,kvb,wo (4x3=12) + 2 norms + MoE 10 -> 24? MoE = router+9+9=19 // KDA total = 15qkv... let me recount: per KDA: q/k/v/g/o: 5*3=15; beta 1; conv 1; norms 2; MoE 19 -> 38 // MLA: q/kva/kvb/o: 12; norms 2; MoE 19 -> 33 // total = 3*38 + 33 = 147 pointers // states/dynamic: x_in, x_out, S[3], cq[3], ck[3], cv[3], ckv_in, kr_in, ckv_out, kr_out, scratch ptrs // scalars: pos, copy_rows, nchunk, x_is_bf16, ... }; ``` 147×8B = 1.2KB param — fine. Instead of a huge hand-managed struct, I'll pass a single `const long long* table` (device int64 array with static ptrs, uploaded once) + fewer dynamic args. Kernel reads table entries from L2 (u64 loads, cached). The inttoptr cast: in CUDA it's trivial (reinterpret_cast from unsigned long long). This simplifies: static args = table ptr (one tensor arg); dynamic args per step = ~15. Table layout: fixed indices. Phases with grid.sync() — cooperative launch. grid = NCTA (choose once: blocks_per_SM = occupancy → grid = SMs × bpsm capped by... at least 1×SMs = 114; try for 2 = 228). Layout of work per phase (grid NCTA): P1 (KDA blk, kind K): - CTA computes xs (fp16 smem): rmsnorm(x)·attn_norm; gsx[18]. - items: 128-col blocks of fused qkvg (N=16384): 128 items... wait with ksplits: I earlier planned ksplit for occupancy: items = (N/128) × ks (ks=2 → 256 items over 228 CTAs → 28 CTAs double → ok). Each item: GEMV over rows_per=576 (9 groups) rows... hmm 576 rows = 1152/2undefinedundefined ✓ groups 9 per split (4.5... 576/64 = 9undefinedundefined ✓). Output to proj scratch fp32, at n0 + col. With ks=2: partial sums atomically added OR stored to separate halves?? partial over k-split → y = item0 + item1 → use atomicAdd (s_proj zeroed first) OR designated item-0 stores... atomics are simplest: zero s_proj in the same phase before?? Zero 16416 floats: takes a moment (16416/228/7 per CTA ~ 10 stores each — cheap) BUT must complete before any item's atomicAdd within the SAME phase — NOT guaranteed!! → zero s_proj at the END of the PREVIOUS phase (each CTA zeroes its slice of the NEXT phase's accumulator): pattern established earlier: during phase X, also init accumulators needed at phase X+2... ugh but s_proj is REUSED per block (4 blocks!) — written in P1 of each block. Zero must happen in the phase before P1 = previous block's P3 or (for block 0) the prologue. So: prologue zeroes s_proj; P3 of block i zeroes s_proj for block i+1 (also out_attn? out_attn used in P2 immediately after P1... P2 of block i zeroes out_moe for +1... let me make a general rule: at the END of each phase (before its grid.sync), CTAs cooperatively zero the accumulators the NEXT phase(s) will need... rather than track precisely: each phase end: zero out_moe (needed at P3) when in P1/P2; zero out_attn (needed at P2) when in P1; zero s_proj when in P3. Simpler concrete scheme: since s_proj writes are NON-atomic if ksplit=1 (each col covered exactly once)! With ks=1, P1 items = 128: 114 CTAs idle-ish?? Using ks=1: 128 items / 228 → 128 CTAs busy... utilization 56% but zero race & no zeroing needed (just store). Hmm — with ks=2: 256 items, full grid, atomics zero+add. Which is better: item work ks1 = 1152 rows × 128B = 147KB per item, util 56% → effective stream 18.9MB × (228/128...)?? no — bandwidth is aggregate over active CTAs: 128 CTAs streaming at full per-CTA rate — per-CTA rate vs SM/CTA assignment: 128 CTAs on 114 SMs → some SMs host 2 CTAs (both active) others 1... per-CTA rate isn't capped by SM count: the TOTAL rate ~ min(#CTAs × rate_per_cta, BW). rate_per_cta ~ 12-16 GB/s (from microbench: 147KB/item at ~1.0-1.1 TB/s aggregate w/ 456 CTAs ~ 2.3 GB/s per CTA?? hmm 1.1e12/456 = 2.4 GB/s per CTA?! and at 228: 4.4 GB/s per CTA... so per-CTA rate is NOT saturated at 228 — bounded by SM sharing/latency). With 128 CTAs: est ~ 128×4.4 = 0.56 TB/s?? That's HALF. So ks=2 with full grid is better. ks=2 needs atomic-or-two-slots for s_proj: use atomicAdd + zeroing pattern. OK. Zeroing scheme final: maintain a tiny "zqueue" convention: each phase, before the grid.sync, some CTAs zero the FP32 accumulators that the NEXT phase's atomics will hit: - Before P1 (block b): nothing (P1 zeroes s_proj for its own block... P1's items atomicAdd s_proj — s_proj must be zero at P1 start → zero at end of PREVIOUS phase (prev block P3 end, or prologue). That's the rule: EVERY phase end (before sync): zero the accumulators used by the FOLLOWING phase: P1 end: zero out_attn (atomics in P2) [P2 atomics: o_proj partials + residual x add — residual handled: out_attn zeroed in P1; P2 start: CTAs atomicAdd residual-x slices; then item atomics] P2 end: zero out_moe (atomics in P3) + zero s_proj for NEXT block's P1 (if any; block 3's P2b end: zero s_proj... wait MLA P2b end → P3 atomics out_moe... and s_proj? s_proj used by P1 of NEXT block (b+1): zero at P3 end of block b. But s_proj is ALSO read... s_proj holds current block's P1 outputs, read during P2 — SAFE to zero at P3 end (no readers in P3)undefinedundefinedundefined ✓. P3 end: zero s_proj (for next P1) + out_attn? out_attn held by... out_attn was finalized in P2; read in P3 (as h for rmsnorm + residual add); after P3, next block's P2 will atomics it again → zero at P3 endundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. So P3 end zeroes: s_proj + out_attn. P2 end zeroes out_moe. P1 end zeroes nothing?? Wait P2's atomics hit out_attn which is zeroed at PREVIOUS P3 end (or prologue for block 0)undefinedundefinedundefined ✓ and prologue zeroes s_proj + out_attn at launch startundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. So: P2b/P2 end: zero out_moe. P3 end: zero s_proj + out_attn. That's clean: each phase does its own zero-of-next. Prologue (block 0 P1 start): zero s_proj + out_attn first — by CTAs at P1 start before gemv items... P2 end: zero out_moe (for P3). P3 end: zero out_attn + s_proj (for next block) + out_moe stays zero until P2 end of next block... wait out_moe was zeroed in P2 end and USED in P3—— it must return to zero before next P3 (block b+1): zero at P2 end of block b+1 covers it IF P2 end comes before every P3undefinedundefinedundefined ✓ P2(b+1) end zeroes out_moe → P3(b+1) atomics hit zeroedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ — BUT there's a RACE: out_moe currently holds block b's FINAL x (read by P1 of b+1 as its input!!). P2(b+1) end zeroing DESTROYS it?? out_moe (block b final) is READ during P1(b+1) and P2(b+1)?? P2(b+1) reads x from... P2 doesn't read x directly... except RESIDUAL: out_attn += x — x = out_moe → P2(b+1) CTAs atomicAdd residual by reading out_moe slices THEN zero out_moe at P2-end — ORDER within P2: residual reads happen at P2 START; zero at P2 ENDundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ all CTAs read before any zeroes?? CTA B in P2 phase reads out_moe at its start; CTA A at ITS end zeroes — if A races through its P2 items faster than B reads residual... A and B are in the SAME PHASE (between same grid.syncs)!! within-phase race again!!! DAMN. Also — CRITICAL: P2a's attention items read the cache from ALIEN ptr when copy_rows>0 (managed buffer rows not yet valid... they never will be read in this step since copy happens in same phase; attention needs the values: read from alien, copy items copy alien→managed, cache-writer writes managed[pos]... and RETURNED state uses managed view with rows [0..pos] = copied + new = validundefinedundefined ✓ chained steps read managedundefinedundefined ✓. NOW — THE SCARY PART — P3 (MoE) structure with atomics, and the P1 input-x staging when x = out_moe fp32. xs staging for phases: P1 stages xn= rmsnorm(x, attn_norm) INTO SMEM only (per CTA). P3 stages xn2 = rmsnorm(h=out_attn, moe_norm). Recurrence/conv don't need xs (small value sets from scratch & states). Manual barrier + normal launch = fewer moving parts. DECISION: manual barrier. Barrier device code: ``` __device__ void gsync(int idx, int gen, volatile int* cnt, volatile int* flg, int ncta) { __syncthreads(); if (threadIdx.x == 0) { __threadfence(); int a = atomicAdd((int*)&cnt[idx], 1); if (a == ncta*(gen+1) - 1) atomicExch((int*)&flg[idx], gen+1); else { while (atomicAdd((int*)&flg[idx], 0) < gen+1) {} } } __syncthreads(); } ``` wait — gen counting: barrier idx reused each launch; counters monotonic: target = (gen+1)*ncta? Each launch bump gen. flag target gen+1. Requires cnt/flg zero initialized at gen=0 startundefinedundefined ✓ (torch zeros at prep; gen increments per launch, tracked host-side, passed as argundefinedundefined ✓). Threadfence + atomic order: producer stores → fence → atomicAdd (release-ish); consumer spin-loads flag then __threadfence before using data — use atomic acquire semantics: reading via atomicAdd(...,0) is not acquire-ordered strictly; add __threadfence() after loop by spinner... In CUDA, global atomics are relaxed; standard practice: __threadfence() after spin exit (acquire side) — add it. Hmm, one concern: spinner CTA's warp0 spins in-syscall; other warps wait at __syncthreads — fine. With grid 228 (2/SM): barriers over 228 CTAs... co-residency: MUST ensure all 228 are resident — with normal launch and grid > resident capacity → DEADLOCK. Occupancy check: write a probe launch or use cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor from host at prep → blocksPerSM = min(2, occ); if occ<2 → grid 114. Since my kernel's smem/regs are fixed at compile: occupancy deterministic — query onceundefinedundefined ✓. Alright, writing mega.py now — full CUDA code. +"""Mega-kernel development harness. Builds the CUDA megakernel, host wrapper,
+and phase-by-phase unit tests against the eager oracle in solution.py.
+
+Phases (one kernel launch per decode step, manual grid barriers):
+  block i in {0..3}, kind K or M:
+  K: P1 proj GEMV (fused qkvg int4 + beta bf16) -> P2 conv+recurrence+o_proj
+     -> P3 MoE
+  M: P1 q/kv_a GEMV (+k_rope rope) -> P2a qlat + attention + cache ops
+     -> P2b combine + Wv + o_proj -> P3 MoE
+"""
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import os

--- /dev/null
+++ b//ephemeral/mega-outputs/runs/20260715_204329_kinetic-claude_kinetic-0715_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/dev/mega_kda.cu
@@ -0,0 +1,276 @@
+// Kimi-Linear W4A16 decode megakernel — ONE launch per decode step.
+// Phase list (each ends with a grid barrier):
+//  0..2   : KDA block0  P1(pack GEMV qkvg+beta) P2(conv+recurrence+o_proj) P3(MoE)
+//  3..5   : KDA block1
+//  6..8   : KDA block2
+//  9..12  : MLA block3  P1(q,kv_a,+k_rope rope) P2a(qlat+attn+copy) P2b(comb+Wv+o) P3(MoE)
+//  13     : final x_out store + invariant zeroing
+#include <torch/extension.h>
+#include <cuda.h>
+#include <cuda_fp16.h>
+#include <cuda_bf16.h>
+#include <cuda_pipeline.h>
+#include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>
… +263 more lines

So barriers: B0:P1,P2,P3 (3), B1 (3), B2 (3), M: P1,P2a,P2b,P3 (4) = 13 barriers [idx 0..12], then final phase no barrier needed. Every phase separatedundefined ✓. The p3 routine gets: norm weight (moe_norm), experts w/s/z ptrs (9 slots routed + shared + router), out_moe, xs/gsx, plus topk smem. Table for MoE per block (KDA slot layout 20..37, MLA 15..32): routed: eg, eu, ed (each w, s, z); shared: sg, su, sd. E.g., eg_w = TAB(blk*38+20) (u8, E×1152×1024 contiguous) index expert e at +(e*1152*1024). Router weight bf16 (64, 2304) row-major. Pass MegaArgs by pointer-to-host-struct → launch wrapper takes the fields as args. Pybind function `mega_step(argdict-ish)`: to keep it simple, wrapper takes: (table, scratch, bscratch, bar, x_in, x_out, S0,S1,S2,cq0..cv2, ckv_in, kr_in, ckv_out, kr_out, pos, gen, nc, copy_rows, phase_limit, ncta) — ~26 args. --- /dev/null
+++ b//ephemeral/mega-outputs/runs/20260715_204329_kinetic-claude_kinetic-0715_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/dev/mega_common.cuh
@@ -0,0 +1,253 @@
+// Kimi-Linear W4A16 decode megakernel. ONE launch per step.
+// Phases (barrier after each):
+//   KDA blk b (0..2): P1 fused qkvg int4 GEMV + beta | P2 conv+recurrence+o_proj | P3 MoE
+//   MLA blk 3       : P1 q,kv_a + k_rope rope | P2a qlat+attention+cache | P2b combine+Wv+o_proj | P3 MoE
+//   final: x_out store + invariant zeroing
+#include <torch/extension.h>
+#include <cuda.h>
+#include <cuda_fp16.h>
+#include <cuda_bf16.h>
+#include <cuda_pipeline.h>
+#include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>
+#include <type_traits>
+#include <vector>
… +240 more lines

In my new GVMap form: r = warp * (ROWS_PAR/8) + rr * ROWS_PAR + rsh: ROWS_PAR=8: r = warp*1 + rr*8 + 0 = warp + rr*8undefinedundefined ✓ same. For COLS=64: ROWS_PAR=16: r = warp*2 + rr*16 + (lane>>4): warp w covers rows {2w, 2w+1} + 16*rr, rr<16?? wait ROWS_PAR=16 iterations: rr in 0..15: r = 2w + rr*16 + rsh: rr*16 exceeds 64 when rr≥4!! rows per group = 64; warp*2 + rr*16: rr up to 3 (rr*16=48 + 2w+1 ≤ 48+15=63) → rr must be < 4!! ROWS_PAR should be the per-warp ITERATION COUNT: for COLS=64: warp covers 2 rows per "pass"; passes = 64/(8 warps × 2) = 4 → 4 iterations: rr ∈ 0..3: r = (2w + rsh) + rr*16. So ROWS_PAR for COLS=64 = 4, and stride between iterations = 16 rows. My formula conflates: let me parametrize differently: per warp: n_iter = 64/(8 warps × rows_per_warp_pass): COLS=128: rows_per_warp_pass=1 → n_iter=8, r = warp + rr*8; COLS=64: rows_per_warp_pass = 2 (lane-half offset) → n_iter = 4, r = warp*2 + rsh + rr*16. Let me rewrite GVMap properly: - COLS=128: rpwp = 1, n_iter = 8, r(rr) = warp + rr*8 - COLS=64: rpwp = 2, n_iter = 4, r(rr) = warp*2 + (lane>>4) + rr*16 Fix the code. Also `CPO`: cp.async ops per thread for a group slab of 64*COLS bytes: 64×64 = 4KB → 256 threads × 16B = 4KB → CPO=1; 64×128 = 8KB → CPO=2. row = tt/(COLS/16), seg = tt%(COLS/16): COLS=64: 4 segs of 16B per rowundefinedundefined ✓ tt<256 → rows 0..63undefinedundefined ✓. COLS=128: 8 segs/row: tt<512: rows = tt/8undefinedundefined ✓ 2 roundsundefinedundefined ✓. Also scrath: `ring` passed as u8* + stride now. SMEM: single ring buffer shared by phases: allocate max needed: STAGES(4) × 8KB = 32KB for COLS=128; for COLS=64: 4 × 4KB = 16KB (ring stride = ring_stride param). Pass ring = the same smem base with different stride. Let me fix these and write a host+test for the P1 phase. +// Phase implementations for the Kimi-Linear megakernel.
+#include "mega_common.cuh"
+
+// ================================================================== //
+// P1 KDA: fused qkvg int4 GEMV (256 items) + beta (1 item)
+// ================================================================== //
+static __device__ void p1_kda(const MegaArgs& A, int blk, __half* xs, float* gsx, float* red, u8* ring) {
+    if (blk == 0) stage_xn<__nv_bfloat16>(A.x_in, TAB_BF(A.tab, T_KW(0, 17)), xs, gsx, red, HIDDEN, 18);
+    else stage_xn<float>(A.scratch + SC_OUTM, TAB_BF(A.tab, T_KW(blk, 17)), xs, gsx, red, HIDDEN, 18);
+    for (int i = threadIdx.x; i < HIDDEN; i += 256) A.scratch[SC_OUTA + i] = 0.f;
+    __syncthreads();
+    int ncta = gridDim.x, cta = blockIdx.x;
+    for (int it = cta; it < 256; it += ncta) {
… +146 more lines

+// ---------------- gemv_rows ----------------
+// Stream a small set of packed rows (ROWS in {16,32}) x N cols (N=2304-like).
+// y[n] += wj * sum_r x[2r,2r+1] (q-z)s. w/sc/zc pre-offset to (row r0, group grp).

+// warp w covers rows warp*(ROWS/8)..+(ROWS/8). z-share per warp = 1/zdiv (caller: 8).
+template <int ROWS>
+static __device__ void gemv_rows(const u8* __restrict__ w, const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ sc,
+                                 const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ zc,
+                                 const __half* __restrict__ xs, float sxo, int N,

+// ================================================================== //
+// P1 MLA: q_proj (48 blocks x ks3) + kv_a c_kv (4+1 x ks3) + kv k_rope raw (ks3)
+// ================================================================== //
+static __device__ void p1_mla(const MegaArgs& A, __half* xs, float* gsx, float* red, u8* ring) {
+    stage_xn<float>(A.scratch + SC_OUTM, TAB_BF(A.tab, T_MW(12)), xs, gsx, red, HIDDEN, 18);
+    for (int i = threadIdx.x; i < HIDDEN; i += 256) A.scratch[SC_OUTA + i] = 0.f;
+    __syncthreads();
+    int ncta = gridDim.x, cta = blockIdx.x;
+    for (int it = cta; it < 159; it += ncta) {
+        if (it < 144) {

But this initializes m to the current token's score — that changes... the online softmax: my init: m = s_cur, z = 1, o = p_cur*c_cur (with p=1 unnormalized). Then tokens processed with exp(s - m) — correct online softmax continuation — GOODundefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. 3. The m/z are per-lane?? NO — m0/m1/z0/z1 are per-warp-head values held redundantly in ALL lanes: the butterfly gives all lanes the same score → each lane updates its own replica of m/z identicallyundefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ no broadcast neededundefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ NICE (I wrote it correctly — the updates happen in every lane uniformly). 4. o_acc j arrays are per-lane covering c 0..15 → o0/1[16] → written to partial slot per lane's rangeundefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. 5. slot layout [slot][16][514]: I wrote pd at `(slot * 16 + h0) * 514` where h0 = warp*2 → head index WITHIN the hg-16 — correct if slot attention span is 16 heads per slot: slot c*2+hg: hg ∈ {0,1} → [slot][h0..h0+1] where h0 = warp*2 ∈ 0..14undefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓ consistent with P2c combine reading (slot*16 + h%16)*514undefinedundefinedundefinedundefined ✓. Final phase zeroing: zero SC_PROJ (16416), SC_OUTA, SC_OUTM, SC_CKNEW+KRNEW (576). x_out copy from OUTM. Write mega_kernel.cu with host launch function (extern "C" via pybind): mega_step(args as tensors/scalars). And the dev python harness that builds the table, allocates, launches with phase_limit, and tests phases. Host wrapper signature: ``` void mega_step(long tab_ptr... — simpler: pass tensors: m.def("mega_step", [](torch::Tensor tab, torch::Tensor x_in, torch::Tensor x_out, torch::Tensor S0, S1, S2, cq0..cv2, torch::Tensor ckv_in, torch::Tensor kr_in, torch::Tensor ckv_out, torch::Tensor kr_out, torch::Tensor scratch, torch::Tensor bscratch, torch::Tensor bar, long pos, long gen, long nc, long copy_rows, long ncop, long phase_limit, long ncta) ``` That assembles MegaArgs and launches. Python side (mega_dev.py): - ModelW: wraps solution.Model; after load, builds tab (torch int64 CUDA), scratch fp32 (SC_FLOATS), bscratch (BS_FLOATS bf16), bar (NBAR*2 int32 zeros), gen counter. - table entries per layout: KDA block b (0..2): [q.w_q, q.scales, q.zeros, k..., v..., g..., o..., beta.weight, conv_w, attn_norm, moe_norm, router.weight, gate.w_q, gate.scales, gate.zeros, up..., down..., s_gate..., s_up..., s_down...] - MLA block 3 at 114: [q(3), kv_a(3), kv_b(3), o(3), attn_norm, moe_norm, router.weight, gate(3), up(3), down(3), s_gate(3), s_up(3), s_down(3)] - step: nctarb = occupancy query once (via cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor(host side C++ function exposed) or just try 228 and 114 fallback on correctness...). For now force 114 (always co-resident with our smem/regs? 37KB smem < 48... registers may exceed... let me query occupancy in the host wrapper: add a C++ function `max_blocks()` returning cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor for the kernel — then python picks ncta = 114 * min(2, maxb). - nc: host python computes: G: pos<4096→32, <8192→64 else 128: nc = ceil(pos/G); cap 164. - gen increments per launch. Cache management (from my earlier design): registry of managed (ckv, kr) buffers keyed by c_kv.data_ptr; on alien: allocate pos+256 rows capacity; copy_rows = pos; ncop = ceil(pos/256). On managed: copy_rows = 0, ncop = 0, in = out buffer, write row pos. Dev harness tests: 1. phase_limit = 0 (K0 P1): compare scratch[SC_PROJ : 16384] vs torch reference qkvg of normed x + beta. 2. phase_limit = 1: compare scratch[SC_OUTA] vs eager h (x + attn out) and S state. 3. phase_limit = 2: compare out_moe (block 0 final) vs eager. 4. run full (phase_limit -1): compare final x_out vs eager full step + new cache. Plus per-phase python-side reference computations using my eager model paths. Write kernel now.undefined

+// Kimi-Linear W4A16 decode megakernel — one launch per decode step.
+#include "mega_common.cuh"
+#include "phases_kda.cuh"

+    __shared__ __half xs[HIDDEN];
+    __shared__ float gsx[32];
+    __shared__ float red[32];
+    __shared__ __align__(16) u8 big[34 * 1024];
+    u8* ring = big;
+
+    #define PHSYNC(i) do { gsync(A, i); if (A.phase_limit == (i)) return; } while (0)

// warp w covers rows warp*(ROWS/8)..+(ROWS/8). z-share per warp = 1/zdiv (caller: 8).
template <int ROWS>
static __device__ void gemv_rows(const u8* __restrict__ w, const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ sc,

So phase_limit=1 did NOT hang under sanitizer; the hang in the normal run may be a race/spin issue. The barrier spin: `while (atomicAdd(flg, 0) < gen+1) {}` — if memory ordering is off, spinners may never see the flag update (livelock), especially with volatile removed! I changed volatile pointers to plain atomicAdd — atomics are fine. But the setter writes flag with atomicExch then spinners read with atomicAdd — both atomics to the same address — should observe. Hmm but there IS a subtle: `A.bar_ref` int* — atomics operate on it. Wait — actually, the hang occurred AFTER phase_limit 0 printed (when running limit=1 normally) but under sanitizer it completed... maybe the hang is a long-running phase (p2_kda is just SLOW?) — p2_kda with 128 items over 114 CTAs, each doing conv + S + o_proj: compute-sanitizer slows it further. Under normal timing p2_kda should take ~ 20µs. The bash loop timed out at 2 min... it ran phase 0 output, then started phase 1 and hung >2min (but probably the timeout hit while phase 2/3 built/loaded torch (each is a separate process, reload + model init ~ 30s each!) — the four processes each take ~40-60s (import torch, build table, model init random quantize?? reference Model init does quantize on ALL weights — reference reset_parameters → expensive!). +// Phase implementations: KDA blocks + MoE (shared by KDA/MLA).
+#pragma once
+#include "mega_common.cuh"
+
+// ================================================================== //
+// P1 KDA: fused qkvg int4 GEMV (256 items) + beta (1 item)
+// ================================================================== //
+static __device__ void p1_kda(const MegaArgs& A, int blk, __half* xs, float* gsx, float* red, u8* ring) {
+    if (blk == 0) stage_xn<__nv_bfloat16>(A.x_in, TAB_BF(A.tab, T_KW(0, 17)), xs, gsx, red, HIDDEN, 18);
+    else stage_xn<float>(A.scratch + SC_OUTM, TAB_BF(A.tab, T_KW(blk, 17)), xs, gsx, red, HIDDEN, 18);
+    for (int i = threadIdx.x; i < HIDDEN; i += 256) A.scratch[SC_OUTA + i] = 0.f;
+    __syncthreads();
+    int ncta = gridDim.x, cta = blockIdx.x;

+"""Kimi-Linear W4A16 hybrid decode unit — single-launch CUDA megakernel.
+
+The ENTIRE per-token forward is one CUDA __global__ launch (cooperative
+grid of persistent CTAs with software grid barriers):
+  * every int4 dequant-GEMV fused (nibble -> fp16 magic -> HFMA2, weights
+    streamed exactly once through cp.async rings; never materialized as bf16)
+  * KDA: fused qkvg GEMV + beta, causal depthwise conv, gated-delta recurrence
+    with S-state update in place, o_proj streamed row-wise
+  * MLA: absorbed attention (qlat = q_nope . W_uk^T then latent-dot softmax
+    over the compressed cache), rope inline, combine + W_v + o_proj
+  * MoE: router + top-8 + 9 fused expert gate/up/down chains with atomics
+  * both RMSNorms and residual adds are fused per-phase.
+
… +390 more lines

Probably phase_limit with an internal barrier that some CTA never reaches?? Wait — all CTAs reach every barrier uniformly... Deadlock scenario: a phase where some CTAs finish their items and reach the barrier early — they spin — others still working — that's a SPIN WAIT (normal). But if a phase has a __syncthreads inside DIVERGENT code where CTAs have different work counts — a CTA-level deadlock (warps within a CTA waiting at different __syncthreads) → whole grid never reaches gsync → global deadlock! Where could CTAs diverge on `__syncthreads()`? - `p2o_kda` item loop: syncs inside item loop body — items per CTA vary (128 items / 114 CTAs: some 1 item, some 2)!!! CTAs with 2 items sync 2× per phase, others 1× — within a CTA it's internally consistent (the whole item loop is uniformly entered per CTA)... __syncthreads is per-CTA: CTA0 does its items with 256 threads syncing per item — count matches within the CTA regardless of other CTAs — NOT a deadlock source. - `if (cta == 0)` beta: fine. - p2b_mla: the attention items + copy + cachewriter: `__syncthreads()` inside `if (it < nc*2) {...}` — flattened? NO — the sync is INSIDE the branch: CTAs executing an attention item sync within it; CTAs doing copy items follow a different path — still CTA-uniform per path (item id uniform per CTA)... The item loop paths diverge WITHIN iteration: ``` for it...: if (it < nc*2) { ...syncs inside... } else if (copy) {... no syncs} else if (cachewriter) {... no syncs} __syncthreads(); // end of iteration — ALL paths hit this? ``` The trailing `__syncthreads()` was inside the loop for `it<nc*2` branch?? Let me check actual code: in p2b_mla I had `__syncthreads();` at the end of the item if-chain inside the for — if some iteration counts differ... it's inside the for loop: every iteration hits it... regardless of branch. OK. - p3_moe: syncs inside item loop and BEFORE at router: all CTAs run router → fine; items: 144 items/114 CTAs — some 2, sync per item — fine per CTA. - p2c_mla: syncs inside the (h, half) item loop — 64 items / 114 → some CTAs 0 items, some 1 — internally consistent. - STAGE_XN: has __syncthreads — called by ALL CTAs in the phase?? MLA P1 calls stage_xn — every CTA in the phase executes it (uniform).undefined ✓ Hmm — WAIT, the deadlock might be much simpler: phase_limit runs that SHORT-CIRCUIT. When phase_limit=N, the kernel returns after barrier N. The NEXT launch (gen+1): all CTAs start P1 of block 0 — barrier targets preserve (gen increments) — no issue... BUT — WAIT, the harness time: warmup calls mm.step(h, R.init_state(...), -1) 3 times, then per-limit: 3 trials × 20 reps. The FIRST limit (0) timing: after warmup at some gen g, launches with gen g+1... — the barrier bar tensor: MM's bar buffer was zeroed ONCE at construction! gen counts up per launch. My diagnostic earlier zeroed mm.bar/mm.gen manually each time — here in prof I DON'T reset — good — continuous gen. So barrier invariant holds... OH WAIT. I SEE THE DEADLOCK!!! mega_dev's step passes `pos` for nc: mega_dev's step computes `nc = self._nc(pos)` where pos = state[3]["c_kv"].shape[0] — state st0's c_kv CHANGES state per call? mega_dev.step MUTATES state[3] into new_state (returns but doesn't modify st0 passed in — actually it does `new_state = list(state); new_state[3] = {...}` — st0 unmodified — BUT... in prof_phases, I pass the SAME st0 repeatedly — alien-detection sees st0's data_ptr → allocates managed buffer ONCE (first call), REGISTERED — next call: copies NOT needed (`ent[2] > pos` → returns managed, copy_rows=0)!! NO copy on later calls — but the managed buffer's rows [0..pos) were NEVER FILLED (copy happened in the FIRST alien call's P2b — phase 11 — the first call WAS phase_limit=-1 (warmup), so rows are fine). But subsequent phase-limited runs with limit < 11 SKIP cache writes while the alien flag is OFF (because registry has the buffers) — the ATTENTION reads rows from managed (not written) — garbage reads — doesn't deadlock, just wrong data. OK not a hang cause. Hmm, so why the hang?! Let me check whether a phase has EXTREME runtime rather than deadlock — e.g., item count explosion: `p2b_mla` total = nc*2 + ncop + 1: nc at 2048 = 64... alien: ncop = 8 → 137 items. Each attention item: chunk G = ceil(2048/64) = 32 tokens × work... per item ~ 130 ops/token × 32 = small. P2a qlat: per item ~ 40 cycles/row × 32 rows... fine. OR — the hang is in run with limit=-1 at ctx=2048?? warmup ran fine before (mm.step full). print showed "--- ctx 2048 (total step: " then hung at limit 0 timing probably. Wait — warmup with R.init_state(cfg, ctx, 0) fresh each is fine. Actually the print statement: `print(f"--- ctx {ctx} (total step: ", end="")` — printed before the limit loop — the failure is in the limit loop FIRST iteration (limit=0) most likely. deadlock in phase-limited paths? limit=0: kernel = P1(b0) + sync(0) + return. P1(b0): stage_xn (all CTAs — has __syncthreads — all fine) + zero + items (256: CTAs do 2-3 items) + beta (cta 0) + gsync. Nothing new vs the earlier FULL runs which passed?! /home/shadeform/kernelbench.com/benchmarks/hard/outputs/gpu_lock:/kbh/lock:rw -w /workspace/problems/01_fp8_gemm nvcr.io/nvidia/tensorrt-llm/release:1.2.1 claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --print --verbose --output-format stream-json --no-session-persistence --settings {"fastMode":false,"alwaysThinkingEnabled":true} --model opus --disallowedTools ExitPlanMode EnterPlanMode AskUserQuestion --add-dir /workspace/problems/01_fp8_gemm -p I need you to optimize an FP8 GEMM for the H100 PCIe (SM90 Hopper, HBM2e, 2.0 TB/s). The reference implementation is in reference.py and your kernel goes in solution.py. You can make whatever mess you want in this directory — scratch files, intermediate kernels, build artifacts, profiling traces — but the final answer has to be in solution.py with the same Model, get_inputs, and get_init_inputs interface as reference.py.  This is a genuine fp8 x fp8 GEMM. Both operands are fp8_e4m3. The activation x is fp8_e4m3 (M, K). The weight is given to you ALREADY quantized: the Model holds `weight` as fp8_e4m3 (N, K) normalized into the e4m3 range, plus `weight_scale` (N,), a per-output-channel dequant scale. You return y = (x @ weight.T) * weight_scale as bf16 (M, N). Read reference.py — it defines this exactly; your Model must register the same `weight` and `weight_scale` buffers so the graded state_dict loads.  To actually score well you must run a real fp8 x fp8 tensor-core MMA (fp8 inputs, fp32 accumulate) and apply the per-channel scale. Upcasting both operands to bf16 and doing a bf16 GEMM is correct but it caps at roughly HALF the roofline — the roofline here is the fp8 peak (~1500 TFLOPS) and bf16 tensor cores run at ~half that. So a bf16 fallback passes correctness but leaves ~2x on the table; the fp8 path is the point.  Correctness tolerance on the bf16 output is ~0.2 abs/rel (fp8 has real accumulation noise, so the bar is loose), but it has to match the reference within that across every shape and the numeric-stress cases. The shapes you must handle are M=4096 N=4096 K=4096 (square aligned, the easy case), M=4096 N=4096 K=4127 (K not a multiple of 128, which forces predicated tails on tile-quantized kernels), M=32 N=8192 K=8192 (skinny M, decode-style, memory-bound), and M=4096 N=14336 K=4096 (Llama-3 up-proj).  This needs to be a real custom kernel. Don't reach for torch._scaled_mm or torch.ops.aten._scaled_mm — they're off-limits and using them fails correctness. Triton tl.dot on fp8, CUDA C++ via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline, inline PTX (mma.sync ... e4m3), or CUTLASS all work — whatever fits. Anything you're uncertain about, look up PTX docs, clone CUTLASS or other reference repos, read library headers, and investigate.  Your flywheel is implement, profile (ncu, nsys, torch.profiler — whatever's useful) and time it with benchmark.py, verify correctness by running `python check.py` and reading the output, then iterate. Don't substitute your own one-off correctness snippets for check.py — it iterates over every shape, your spot-check almost certainly won't. If `python check.py` hasn't printed PASS, you're not done. Take as long as you need to actually push the number up.  Workspace isolation note: you are already running inside the archive-local problem workspace, /workspace/problems/01_fp8_gemm. Write the final answer to solution.py in the current directory only. The source repository's problems/ tree is not mounted.  Container note: inside this run, the visible workspace path is /workspace/problems/01_fp8_gemm. The source repository, old runs, leaderboards, and host harness memory are not mounted. Container network mode is bridge. Run all Python through `uv run ...` so you use the workspace uv environment; it is built from the same uv.lock as the official scoring environment. The container image's system python has a different torch build and is NOT the scoring environment.

reasoning
Interesting archaeology — there's ANOTHER containerized run sharing this GPU (the ps output shows a docker container from a different benchmark problem running simultaneously! "20260715_203916... 01_fp8_gemm" — that's a DIFFERENT tenant process competing for the GPU!). +cfg = R.build_config({"n_experts": 64})
+ref = R.Model(cfg).cuda().eval()
+sol = SOL.Model(cfg)
