So limit 1 completed with ncta 228 once... but now hangs consistently. SO SOMETHING NONDETERMINISTIC about it — co-residency deadlock OCCASIONAL at 228!! THE OTHER TENANT: when it's using GPU, my 228-CTA persistent kernel with spin barrier DEADLOCKS because not all CTAs get scheduled together (spinning CTAs never yield, pending ones starve). THE SPIN BARRIER IS TOXIC ON A SHEARED GPU!!! And whether it works is LUCK of the moment — explains the occasional huge latencies too (barriers eventually resolve when the other tenant yields). This is a huge liability: the GRADED environment may also share the GPU... or not. The benchmark fails catastrophically if it deadlocks. My spin barrier needs robustness against incomplete co-residency! Mitigations: 1. Reduce ncta to 1/SM (114): with occ 2 we still launch 114 CTAs — more likely all resident — helps but no guarantee. 2. **Make the barrier NON-BLOCKING-SPIN with forward progress**: use the `atomicAdd` arrival count + spin with a note: CTAs spin only a bounded time then... they can't exit early (correctness). 3. **Use cooperative groups grid.sync() via cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel**: cooperative launch GUARANTEES all CTAs are co-resident BEFORE start (the driver enforces it — if resources short, it fails/errors instead of deadlocking... cooperative launch waits for co-residency or returns an error). The CTA scheduling handles other tenants... cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel checks co-residency at launch and errors if it can't schedule. That converts silent deadlock into a catchable error (I'd pick ncta by occupancy). 4. Keep spin barrier but pick ncta conservatively (114 = 1/SM): co-residency likely even shared. The other tenant shares the GPU at CTA granularity — with 114 CTAs of my own and theirs... still possible to starve if their grid is huge and mine never fits 114 at once. Hmm but GPU scheduling: new CTAs are placed as old retire; with spinning CTAs never retiring — if the other tenant launches a huge grid after some of my CTAs placed, mine might fill the rest... deadlock only if at least one of my CTAs never launches because GPUs are full of theirs AND mine already placed spinners never die — YES deadlock possible in principle. BUT torch/benchmark processes serialize within my own steps; the foreign tenant is the wildcard. Honestly, in production decode kernels people use cooperative launches for exactly this. Since I control the launcher, use cooperative launch + grid.sync(). That ALSO likely uses a hardware/driver-supported barrier (fast). Need: compile with -rdc=true (cg::this_grid().sync() requires it historically; on newer CUDA it works without? For sm_90 CUDA 12: cg grid sync needs rdc — include <cooperative_groups.h> and compile -rdc=truendefined... load_inline with nvcc driver links fine. Fallback plan if rdc/cooperative is finicky: use cooperative LAUNCH (guarantees residency) + MY spin barrier (safe under guaranteed co-residency)! Cooperative launch itself = the guarantee I need; my spin barrier is then fine. cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel is a runtime API I already call in the launcher.PUT THEIR THAT WIN: convert `mega_kernel<<<...>>>` to cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel(...), keep my barriers, and occupancy-query max ACTIVE blocks for sizing. If cooperative launch errors due to the other tenant (it fails with cudaErrorCooperativeLaunchTooLarge gracefully?) — actually it doesn't query external load — it validates only MY occupancy: if other kernels occupy SMs when I launch mine cooperatively — it might still fail to place all → cudaError... whatever!! With 114 CTAs at 256 thr/40KB — co-residency is trivial I think. Fine. Implement now: host launcher uses cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel with arg pointers. Keep spin barrier. Also honor: cooperative launch requires device support (query cudaDevAttrCooperativeLaunch — H100 yes). Also — the deadlock explains a LOT of my earlier flaky slowness. Let me now: 1. Switch launcher to cooperative launch. 2. Optionally keep ncta=228 with occupancy 2. 3. Re-validate check + benchmark. With 228 CTAs ALL reading the same 295KB simultaneously, the L2 serves 67MB — at L2 bw ~ 6-8 TB/s = ~10µs AGGREGATE, but each CTA sees its 295KB at L2-load issue rate: per SM per cycle ~ 32B L1 return → 295KB/32B = 9.2K cycles = 5.3µs — fine. Each LDG ~ some cycles issue... ~ 10-15µs. And shared-mem xs reads — bf16→float conversions... all ALU. TOTAL ~ 60µs incl. topk's serial 64×(64+...) with shared writes + serial exp calls (~ 1-2µs fine). So router+topk is burdened by bad codegen (element loads). FIX with vectorized uint4 loads for Wr + a proper GEMV formulation: lane covers 2304/32=72 → uint4 loads (16B = 8 bf16 per lane-load): 9 uint4 loads per lane per e → compute via bf16x2 unpack. ~ 10x fewer instructions → expect ~ 6µs.
